message:
fcp-raptorq/decode: reject malformed symbols at ingress before touching the decoder buffer

`RaptorQDecoder::add_symbol` accepted any `data: Vec<u8>` from the
caller and forwarded it into the duplicate-check / buffering path,
implicitly relying on RaptorQ's block-decode step to eventually notice
a size mismatch. That works for correctness but has three problems:

1. A malformed symbol of the wrong byte length can still grow the
   `received` map (indexed by ESI), nudging memory pressure and
   triggering other state — including the duplicate-hit short-circuit
   — against inputs that were never valid RaptorQ symbols to begin
   with.
2. The downstream error surface comes from deep inside the raptorq
   crate's decode step, so operators see a generic block-decode
   failure instead of a specific "wrong-size symbol at ingress" signal.
3. A noisy peer could use wrong-length symbols to poison the ESI set
   with otherwise-valid indexes, causing later correct symbols for
   the same ESI to be silently dropped by the duplicate check.

Fix: check `data.len() == usize::from(self.symbol_size)` before any
other work in `add_symbol`, and on mismatch return
`DecodeError::InvalidSymbol { reason: "symbol size mismatch: expected
{expected_len} bytes, got {actual} bytes" }`. The size check sits
above the duplicate-skip block, so malformed symbols neither allocate
buffer state nor populate the ESI set.

New test `decoder_rejects_malformed_symbol_before_buffering`:
construct an `ObjectTransmissionInformation { max_transfer_length:
256, symbol_size: 64, ... }`, feed a 63-byte `vec![0xAA; 63]` at
ESI 0, and assert both:
* `add_symbol(...)` returns `Err(DecodeError::InvalidSymbol { .. })`, and
* `decoder.received_count() == 0` — i.e. nothing was buffered.

The timeout check is still evaluated first so that "we already timed
out" wins over "this symbol is also malformed", preserving the
existing timeout-is-terminal invariant.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
